metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` with the same `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` call the inbound differ uses (inbound_differ.py:210) for the description comparison in reconcile_check — the FieldSanitizer is send-side only and emits warning logs on truncation.
evidence: inbound_differ.py:210: `local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)` — inbound differ uses the ADF module directly, not FieldSanitizer
_backend.py:105-119: FieldSanitizer Protocol docstring says 'Defend the backend's hard limits on field values (send-side only)'
adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:158-181: `_sanitize_description` calls `logger.warning(...)` on truncation — a side effect inappropriate in a comparison context
The plan asserts this 'mirrors the differ's description parity rule [rebar:625b]' but the differ uses fit_text_to_adf_limit directly, not FieldSanitizer
findings.finding: `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` is documented as a 'send-side only' sanitizer and emits a WARNING log on truncation; the inbound differ — the stated parity reference — uses `_load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit` directly (inbound_differ.py:210), NOT `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description`. Using the send-side sanitizer in reconcile_check's comparison path would introduce spurious warning logs on every reconcile-check run for any description near the ADF limit, contradicting the 'no side-effects' expectation of a pure comparison.
findings.location: Approach section — 'description: compare the port-sanitized local text (`FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description`) against the mapper-decoded remote text'
scenarios: Any reconcile-check run against a Jira snapshot containing a description near the ADF character limit will emit spurious warning logs, alarming operators with false 'description truncated' signals.
If a future backend's FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description raises (per Protocol contract: 'raises on an unfixable value'), reconcile_check would throw instead of reporting a discrepancy.
findings.suggested_fix: In the description comparison, call `inbound_mapper.map_remote_to_local(jira_issue)["description"]` for the remote side and use `fit_text_to_adf_limit(str(local_val or ""))` (via the same lazy adf loader the inbound differ uses) for the local side, preserving the rstrip tolerance — matching the inbound differ pattern at inbound_differ.py:210.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the description comparison in reconcile_check should call `_load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit` directly (matching inbound_differ.py:210's read-side pattern) rather than routing through `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` (a send-side port), and update the Approach wording accordingly.
evidence: Plan text: 'compare the port-sanitized local text (FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description) against the mapper-decoded remote text'
_backend.py:106 docstring: 'Defend the backend's hard limits on field values (send-side only)'
inbound_differ.py:210: 'local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)' — the stated model uses direct adf call, not FieldSanitizer
adapters/jira/backend.py:59-60: sanitize_description delegates to jira_fields._sanitize_description which also calls fit_text_to_adf_limit — same underlying function, different call path
findings.finding: The plan specifies using `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` (a send-side port, docstring: 'Defend the backend's hard limits on field values (send-side only)') to sanitize the local description before comparison, but the inbound_differ — the explicitly named model to mirror — calls `_load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)` directly (inbound_differ.py:210) without going through the FieldSanitizer port. Routing a read-side comparison through a send-side port is a golden-hammer misapplication: it forces a port dependency on reconcile_check for what is a pure in-memory text truncation, and it re-uses an abstraction outside its documented semantic scope.
findings.location: Approach section — description bullet: 'compare the port-sanitized local text (FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description)'
scenarios: A future backend whose FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description does more than just truncate (e.g. strips markup for a non-ADF backend) would silently alter reconcile_check's comparison semantics — a read-side check should not be coupled to a send-side transformation contract.
Injecting FieldSanitizer into reconcile_check adds a second port dependency (alongside InboundMapper) for a function that inbound_differ obtains without any port injection.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description' with '_load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit' in the description bullet of the Approach, matching inbound_differ.py:210 exactly. The mapper-decoded remote text is already plain-text after map_remote_to_local, so the comparison becomes: `fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val).rstrip() == (mapped_remote_description or '').rstrip()` — no port injection needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the description comparison path: use the pure ADF-fit helper (equivalent to `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit`) accessed via the canonical differ's established route, NOT `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` (which is send-side and has warning side-effects); update the Approach text and AC2 to name the exact function/method.
evidence: adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:158-180: `_sanitize_description` calls `_fit_description_to_adf_limit` AND emits a logger.warning when truncation occurs — this is a side effect not appropriate for a read-only reconcile-check comparison path
adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py:560-583: today's differ applies `_load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)` (the pure fitting function) then rstrips — NOT `sanitize_description`
Plan text: 'compare the port-sanitized local text (`FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description`)' — this differs from what the differ actually uses (the bare ADF fit helper)
findings.finding: The description comparison plan — 'compare the port-sanitized local text (`FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description`) against the mapper-decoded remote text with the same rstrip tolerance' — is an unverified behavioral equivalence claim. `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` is the send-side truncator (`_sanitize_description` → `fit_text_to_adf_limit`); after canonicalization via `map_remote_to_local`, the remote text is already plain text (ADF decoded, no fit applied). Today's code applies `fit_text_to_adf_limit` to the LOCAL value and compares against the ADF-decoded REMOTE value. The new path applies `sanitize_description` (= fit) to the local value and compares against the mapper-decoded remote value — which is structurally the same, BUT `sanitize_description` also enforces the description-must-be-str contract and emits a warning, making it a side-effectful call on the read path. The plan does not acknowledge this distinction.
findings.location: Approach section — 'description: compare the port-sanitized local text (`FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description`) against the mapper-decoded remote text with the same rstrip tolerance'
scenarios: Every reconcile-check run on a ticket whose local description exceeds the ADF limit will emit a spurious truncation warning even though reconcile_check never writes anything — misleading operators.
If `sanitize_description` raises on a non-str description (its contract check), reconcile_check will abort on malformed local data rather than reporting a discrepancy.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `backend.sanitizer`'s underlying pure fit (e.g. call `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` via a port helper, or access it through the mapper's decoded output directly with rstrip) rather than `sanitize_description`. Alternatively, expose a separate `FieldSanitizer.fit_description(description: str) -> str` method that is pure (no warning, no raise) for comparison use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for the extended neutrality gate: either a source-text scan for `adapters/jira` path-string literals in `reconcile_check.py` (mirroring the `AcliClient(` text scan at test line 52), or confirm that importing `reconcile_check` under the test runner itself imports the vendor module and a module-scope denylist import check would catch it — and document which approach the AC relies on.
evidence: test_backend_neutrality.py:107-116: `_module_level_imported_names` uses `ast.parse` and iterates `tree.body` for top-level `ImportFrom` nodes only — function-nested imports are excluded by design
test_backend_neutrality.py:119-130: the parametrized test only checks module-level imports of mapper entrypoints, not by-path path literals
reconcile_check.py:61-75: the by-path loads are inside `_load_outbound_fields()` and `_load_adf()` functions — invisible to the AST module-level scanner
No existing path-literal or source-text scan for `adapters/jira` path strings exists in the gate file
ABSENCE: no grep/read evidence of a path-literal detector in test_backend_neutrality.py
findings.finding: The plan's AC asserts the extended neutrality case in `test_backend_neutrality.py` will fail if `reconcile_check.py` 're-grows an `adapters.jira` import or by-path vendor sibling load', but the existing gate only detects MODULE-LEVEL imports via AST (`_module_level_imported_names` at test line 107-116). The `_load_sibling(...)` calls are INSIDE functions, so the AST module-level import scanner cannot detect them. No path-literal scanner currently exists in the test file. The plan never specifies what mechanism the extended gate case will use to detect function-nested by-path loads.
findings.location: AC bullet 1: 'verified by the extended case in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py`, whose reconcile_check carve-out comment is removed'; also Testing section: 'Gate: the extended neutrality case fails if `reconcile_check.py` re-grows an `adapters.jira` import or by-path vendor sibling load.'
scenarios: If the plan's AC simply removes the carve-out comment and adds reconcile_check.py to the existing AST import parametrize list, it will silently pass even if a developer later re-adds a `_load_sibling('x', 'adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py')` inside a function — the regression gate is ineffective.
The AC's 'verified by the extended case' claim is unbacked because the mechanism to detect function-nested by-path loads is never specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a source-text gate case (like the existing `AcliClient(` scan at test line 47-56) that asserts `'adapters/jira/' not in text` for `reconcile_check.py`, which would catch any by-path `_load_sibling` pointing into the vendor adapter directory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite declaration that this story must not be implemented until story 625b (`assignee_identity` canonical key in `InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local`) is merged; or alternatively, scope the assignee comparison to use the existing `assignee_identity` key only if present and fall back to the current membership-matching logic against a plain-string `assignee` for backward compatibility during the transition.

findings.finding: The plan's assignee comparison path depends on a new 'assignee_identity' key emitted by InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local that does not yet exist in the current implementation. The current _map_jira_to_local_fields (inbound_fields.py:177-184) returns only a plain 'assignee' string (via _extract_jira_field_value returning displayName), not the {display, email, account_id} dict the plan requires. This key is being added by the open prerequisite story rebar:625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f, but that story is still in planning. If this plan is implemented while 625b is undelivered, the assignee membership-matching path will silently fail (KeyError or empty dict), breaking the assignee comparison for every bound pair.
findings.location: Approach section, assignee bullet: 'neutral membership matching against the canonical assignee_identity dict {display, email, account_id} [rebar:625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f]'
scenarios: Implementation order: if this reconcile_check story is implemented before 625b lands, _compare_pair calls map_remote_to_local and then looks up 'assignee_identity' — which is absent — resulting in a KeyError or silently treating every assignee as unmatched.

Partial delivery: if 625b is delivered but only partially (e.g., outbound_differ gets assignee_identity but inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields is not updated), reconcile_check's inbound_mapper.map_remote_to_local still won't emit the key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the assignee matching rule explicitly in the Approach (e.g., 'match if any of {account_id, email, display} compares equal between the local assignee_identity and the canonical remote value, with account_id taking precedence when present') rather than deferring it entirely to the prerequisite citation.

findings.finding: The assignee matching algorithm is described only by structure ('neutral membership matching against the canonical `assignee_identity` dict `{display, email, account_id}`') and is fully deferred to [rebar:625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f], which this plan cannot confirm defines the rule. An executor reading only this plan cannot determine whether matching is 'any field equals', 'best-available-field precedence', or 'all fields must agree', creating an ambiguity that could produce a non-equivalent replacement for `_assignee_matches`.
findings.location: Approach section, assignee bullet; Testing edge/failure bullet ('assignee matching across accountId / email / displayName local values and both-empty')
scenarios: An executor implements 'all-fields-must-match' semantics while the canonical differ uses 'any-field-matches', producing a regression the behavior tests may not catch if the test fixtures happen to have all three fields aligned.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the assignee bullet: 'Membership match rule: prefer account_id equality; fall back to email, then displayName — mirroring the rule defined in [rebar:625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f]; if both local and remote produce empty/None for all three fields, treat as equal (no drift).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `__main__.py` to Scope (or explicitly acknowledge it): update `_run_reconcile_check` to resolve `backend.inbound` and pass it to `reconcile_check()`, matching the injection pattern used by `run_differs.py` for `inbound_mapper`.
evidence: Plan Scope lists: reconcile_check.py, test_backend_neutrality.py, tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ — __main__.py is absent.
__main__.py:154: `report = rc_mod.reconcile_check(local_tickets, jira_snapshot, binding_store)` — 3-arg call with no inbound_mapper.

After canonicalization, reconcile_check internally resolves `select_backend(load_config()).inbound` (the lazy fallback pattern from outbound_differ.py:450-454). If the parameter is optional the call still works, but __main__.py never passes the resolved backend's inbound mapper — meaning the lazy-config path is taken on every CLI invocation rather than the injected-mapper path the AC tests exercise.
If instead inbound_mapper is a required parameter (no lazy fallback), __main__.py breaks at runtime without a code change.
findings.finding: `__main__.py`'s `_run_reconcile_check` calls `rc_mod.reconcile_check(local_tickets, jira_snapshot, binding_store)` — the only non-test external caller of `reconcile_check()` — but `__main__.py` is not listed in Scope and not mentioned as a consumer to update.
scenarios: CLI `--mode reconcile-check` invocation in production: `_run_reconcile_check` calls `reconcile_check()` without `inbound_mapper`, triggering either a TypeError (required param) or a silent lazy-backend-resolve that bypasses injection.
The neutrality gate AC ('verified by the extended case in test_backend_neutrality.py') does not cover __main__.py's call site — a regression there would be invisible to the gate.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_run_reconcile_check`, after loading the binding store, add: `from rebar_reconciler._backend_registry import select_backend; from rebar.config import load_config; inbound_mapper = select_backend(load_config()).inbound` and pass `inbound_mapper=inbound_mapper` to `rc_mod.reconcile_check(...)`. Add `__main__.py` to Scope.

Plan scope says 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ (reconcile-check comparison behavior tests)' — ambiguous whether this covers updating the EXISTING orchestrate test file or only creating new tests.
ABSENCE: `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/orchestrate/test_reconcile_check.py` is not explicitly named in Scope, unlike how the prerequisite ticket 625b named its new characterization file explicitly.
findings.finding: The existing `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/orchestrate/test_reconcile_check.py` (~15 call sites: `rc_mod.reconcile_check(local_tickets, jira_snapshot, store)`) is an unenumerated consumer that will be affected by the change — its snapshot fixtures use flat string shapes for status/priority (e.g. `"status": "To Do"`) that the new `InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local` path must handle, and its call signature must accommodate any new parameter.
scenarios: After canonicalization, `_compare_pair` calls `inbound_mapper.map_remote_to_local({'priority': 'Highest', ...})`. The existing `_map_jira_to_local_fields` implementation uses `_extract_jira_field_value(jira_fields, 'priority')` which handles both plain strings and `{name: ...}` objects — so the mapper likely handles flat shapes gracefully. But this needs to be verified/acknowledged in the plan.
If `reconcile_check()` gains a required `inbound_mapper` parameter, all existing test calls break at collection time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm in the Approach section that the Jira `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` implementation applies `fit_text_to_adf_limit` semantics (or is exactly `fit_text_to_adf_limit`), so the description comparison behavior is identical to today's; if not, specify that the description path should use `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` only when it subsumes `fit_text_to_adf_limit`, otherwise keep `fit_text_to_adf_limit` as the canonicalization step after decoding the remote ADF.
evidence: Plan: 'compare the port-sanitized local text (FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description) against the mapper-decoded remote text with the same rstrip tolerance, replacing the _extract_jira_field/fit_text_to_adf_limit pair'
Current reconcile_check.py:242: `local_text = adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit(str(local_val or ''))`

outbound_fields.py:566-567: `if field_name == 'description' and isinstance(local_val, str): local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)` — this is the behavior that prevents phantom updates for oversized descriptions
_backend.py:117 defines `sanitize_description(self, description: str) -> str` abstractly — no specification that it must apply fit_text_to_adf_limit semantics
Plan does not confirm that the Jira FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description implementation calls fit_text_to_adf_limit
findings.finding: The description comparison semantics may diverge from the current behavior: the plan uses `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` on the local text, whereas the current code uses `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` (reconcile_check.py:242). The `FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description` protocol method is defined abstractly in `_backend.py:117` with no guarantee that it applies the same ADF byte-limit truncation that `fit_text_to_adf_limit` performs. If the Jira implementation of `sanitize_description` truncates at a different boundary, or does not truncate at all, the 'oversized ADF-fitted description does not read as drift' edge case (explicitly listed in Testing) would produce a different match/mismatch decision — a regression in AC2 ('discrepancy decisions are identical to today's for Jira').
findings.location: Approach section — 'compare the port-sanitized local text (FieldSanitizer.sanitize_description) against the mapper-decoded remote text with the same rstrip tolerance'; Testing edge case 'an oversized ADF-fitted description does not read as drift'
scenarios: A local description exceeds the ADF byte limit; current code truncates via fit_text_to_adf_limit before comparing, so it matches the stored Jira value; new code uses sanitize_description which may not truncate — mismatch reported as a false discrepancy on every bound ticket with a long description

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
